title: Check Isomorphic Strings
description: Two strings are isomorphic if characters can be replaced to get the other with 1-1 mapping.

examples.explanation: Step 1: Lengths equal (3=3). Step 2: Unique chars: s1={'e','g'}, s2={'a','d'}, both size 2. Step 3: Generate permutations of {'a','d'}: [('a','d'), ('d','a')]. Step 4: Test mapping1: {'e':'a', 'g':'d'}, transform 'egg'→'add', matches s2='add'→RETURN TRUE. (Mapping2 would give 'egg'→'daa' which doesn't match, but we already found success).
Step 1: Lengths equal (3=3). Step 2: Unique chars: s1={'f','o'}, s2={'b','a','r'}, sizes 2≠3→RETURN FALSE immediately (no bijection possible between sets of different sizes).

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n! * n)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible character mappings between the two strings and check each one for validity. This approach is impractical for any reasonable input size but demonstrates the exhaustive search concept.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Isomorphic String Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking if two strings are isomorphic by testing all possible character mappings.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the isomorphic strings problem. The intuition is that two strings are isomorphic if there exists a one-to-one mapping (bijection) between their characters that transforms one string into the other. Since we don't know what this mapping might be, we try ALL possible mappings between the character sets of the two strings. For strings of length n with k unique characters, there are k! possible bijections (permutations) between the character sets. For each mapping, we check if applying it to the first string produces exactly the second string. The algorithm works by: 1) Extracting unique characters from both strings, 2) Generating all permutations of mappings from s1 chars to s2 chars, 3) For each mapping, transforming s1 and comparing to s2, 4) Returning true if any mapping works, false otherwise. Time complexity is O(k! * n) where k is number of unique characters (worst case k=n, so O(n! * n)). Space is O(n) for storing mappings and transformed strings. This connects to L1 by using the permutation generation as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include: not handling different length strings (should return false), not ensuring one-to-one mapping (bijection requirement), and inefficient permutation generation. Real code pattern: def is_isomorphic_brute(s1, s2): if len(s1) != len(s2): return False; unique1 = list(set(s1)); unique2 = list(set(s2)); if len(unique1) != len(unique2): return False; for perm in permutations(unique2): mapping = dict(zip(unique1, perm)); transformed = ''.join(mapping[c] for c in s1); if transformed == s2: return True; return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Lengths equal (3=3). Step 2: Unique chars: s1={'e','g'}, s2={'a','d'}, both size 2. Step 3: Generate permutations of {'a','d'}: [('a','d'), ('d','a')]. Step 4: Test mapping1: {'e':'a', 'g':'d'}, transform 'egg'→'add', matches s2='add'→RETURN TRUE. (Mapping2 would give 'egg'→'daa' which doesn't match, but we already found success).
Step 1: Lengths equal (3=3). Step 2: Unique chars: s1={'f','o'}, s2={'b','a','r'}, sizes 2≠3→RETURN FALSE immediately (no bijection possible between sets of different sizes).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Character Mapping Permutations
ladders.desc: Generate all possible one-to-one mappings between two character sets.
ladders.explanation: This is the most computationally intensive part of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to test ALL possible ways characters from set A could map to set B in a one-to-one fashion, we need to generate all permutations of set B and pair them with set A. For sets of size k, there are k! permutations. Each permutation represents a complete bijection where the i-th element of set A maps to the i-th element of the permuted set B. The algorithm: 1) Take two lists: listA (unique chars from s1) and listB (unique chars from s2), 2) If lengths differ, return empty (no bijection possible), 3) Generate all permutations of listB using backtracking/Heap's algorithm, 4) For each permutation, create a dictionary mapping listA[i]→permutation[i]. Time: O(k!) to generate permutations. Space: O(k!) to store all permutations (though we can generate on-the-fly). This feeds into L0 by providing the mappings to test. Common mistakes: generating permutations of wrong size, not handling duplicate characters properly (should work with sets), and inefficient permutation algorithms. Real code pattern: def generate_mappings(chars1, chars2): if len(chars1) != len(chars2): return []; perms = permutations(chars2); return [dict(zip(chars1, p)) for p in perms].

examples.trace: Step 1: Both size 2. Step 2: Permutations of ['a','d']: [('a','d'), ('d','a')]. Step 3: Map to ['e','g']: First: e→a, g→d. Second: e→d, g→a.
Step 1: Both size 1. Step 2: Only permutation of ['y'] is ['y']. Step 3: Map: x→y.

testCases.expected: [] after deduplication to ['a','b'] vs ['c','d','e'] size mismatch

ladders.title: Core Logic — Apply Character Mapping and Verify String Transformation
ladders.desc: Given a mapping dictionary, transform one string and check if it equals another.
ladders.explanation: This is the core verification logic. The intuition is simple: if we have a candidate mapping from characters of s1 to characters of s2, we can apply it to every character in s1 to produce a transformed string. If this transformed string exactly equals s2, then this mapping successfully demonstrates the strings are isomorphic. The algorithm: 1) Input: strings s1, s2, and mapping dict, 2) If lengths differ, return false, 3) For each index i, get mapped_char = mapping[s1[i]], 4) If at any point mapped_char ≠ s2[i], return false, 5) After processing all characters, return true. Time: O(n) for scanning the string. Space: O(1) extra space. This feeds into L1 by being the verification step for each generated mapping. Common mistakes: not checking all characters, assuming mapping covers all characters (should cover at least those in s1), and not handling missing keys in mapping. Real code pattern: def verify_mapping(s1, s2, mapping): if len(s1) != len(s2): return False; for i in range(len(s1)): if mapping.get(s1[i]) != s2[i]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Lengths 3=3. Step 2: i=0: s1[0]='e'→mapping['e']='a', s2[0]='a'✓. Step 3: i=1: s1[1]='g'→'d', s2[1]='d'✓. Step 4: i=2: s1[2]='g'→'d', s2[2]='d'✓. All match→TRUE.
Step 1: Lengths 3=3. Step 2: i=0: 'a'→'d', s2[0]='d'✓. Step 3: i=1: 'b'→'e', s2[1]='e'✓. Step 4: i=2: 'c'→'g', but s2[2]='f'≠'g'→RETURN FALSE.

ladders.title: Building Block — Extract Unique Characters from String
ladders.desc: Identify all distinct characters in a string and represent them as an ordered list.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed because our brute force approach works with character sets, not individual string positions. The intuition: to generate mappings between characters of two strings, we need to know WHAT characters need to be mapped. We extract the unique characters from each string, which gives us the domain (from s1) and codomain (from s2) for our mapping function. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty set, 2) Iterate through each character in the string, 3) Add character to set (sets automatically handle duplicates), 4) Convert set to list for consistent ordering. Time: O(n) to scan string. Space: O(k) where k≤n is number of unique chars. This feeds into L2 by providing the character sets for mapping generation. Common mistakes: using list instead of set (won't remove duplicates), not preserving order (order doesn't matter for mapping generation but consistency helps), and misunderstanding Unicode characters. Real code pattern: def unique_chars(s): return list(set(s)).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty set {}. Step 2: Char 'h'→set {'h'}. Step 3: 'e'→{'h','e'}. Step 4: 'l'→{'h','e','l'}. Step 5: 'l' (duplicate)→set unchanged {'h','e','l'}. Step 6: 'o'→{'h','e','l','o'}. Convert to list→['h','e','l','o'] (order may vary).
Step 1: Empty set {}. Step 2-4: Process 'a','a','a'→set {'a'}. Step 5-7: Process 'b','b','b'→set {'a','b'}. Convert to list→['a','b'].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through String Characters
ladders.desc: Process each character in a string sequentially from first to last.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used throughout the solution: to extract unique characters, apply mappings, and compare strings. The intuition is that strings are sequences, and we need to access each element (character) one by one to perform operations on them. The algorithm: 1) Get string length n, 2) Use index i from 0 to n-1, 3) Access character at position i using s[i], 4) Perform desired operation on that character. Time: O(n) for full iteration. Space: O(1) for index variable. This feeds into L3 as the mechanism to scan the string for unique character extraction. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (using ≤n instead of <n), modifying string during iteration, and not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(s)): char = s[i]; # process char. Or: for char in s: # process char directly.

examples.trace: Step 1: Length n=3. Step 2: i=0→s[0]='c'. Step 3: i=1→s[1]='a'. Step 4: i=2→s[2]='t'. Done.
Step 1: Length n=1. Step 2: i=0→s[0]='a'. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a String?
ladders.desc: Understanding strings as immutable sequences of characters.
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute prerequisite concept. A string is a data structure that represents text as a sequence of characters. Intuition: Think of a string like a train where each carriage is a character, and carriages are arranged in a specific order. Key properties: 1) Strings are immutable (cannot change individual characters directly), 2) They have length (number of characters), 3) Characters can be letters, digits, symbols, or spaces, 4) Positions are indexed starting from 0. Algorithmically: We can access characters by position (s[0] is first character), get length with len(s), and create new strings by concatenation. Time/space: Accessing by index is O(1), getting length is O(1) typically. This feeds into L4 as the basic object we iterate through. Common mistakes: confusing string with list (strings can't be modified in-place), off-by-one errors with indices, and not understanding Unicode characters. Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; first_char = s[0]; length = len(s); empty_string = ''.

examples.output: Length 3, characters: 'd' at index 0, 'o' at 1, 'g' at 2
examples.trace: Step 1: The string contains 3 characters. Step 2: Position 0 holds 'd'. Step 3: Position 1 holds 'o'. Step 4: Position 2 holds 'g'. Step 5: s[0]='d', s[1]='o', s[2]='g', len(s)=3.
Step 1: The string has no characters. Step 2: Length is 0. Step 3: Trying to access any index (like s[0]) would cause an error.

testCases.expected: Length 1 (Unicode emoji counts as one character), s[0]='🎉'

approaches.name: Two HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two hash maps to track bidirectional mappings between characters of both strings. Check consistency while iterating through the strings to ensure one-to-one correspondence.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is character set size (typically O(1) for ASCII)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Isomorphic Strings with Two Hash Maps
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two hash maps to verify bidirectional character mappings between two strings.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that for two strings to be isomorphic, there must be a one-to-one correspondence between their characters. This means each character in string s must map to exactly one character in string t, and vice versa. Using two hash maps ensures this bidirectional consistency: map_s_t tracks mappings from s to t, while map_t_s tracks mappings from t to s. As we iterate through both strings simultaneously, we check if the current character in s already has a mapping. If it does, we verify it maps to the current character in t. If not, we create a new mapping, but first check if the current character in t is already mapped to a different character in s using map_t_s. This prevents multiple characters in s from mapping to the same character in t, ensuring the one-to-one property. The algorithm runs in O(n) time, where n is the length of the strings, since we traverse each character once. Space complexity is O(k), where k is the size of the character set (typically O(1) for ASCII). This approach directly implements the definition of isomorphism by enforcing both forward and backward uniqueness. Common mistakes include using only one hash map (which fails for cases like 'ab' and 'aa'), not handling strings of different lengths, and incorrect iteration bounds. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. If lengths differ, return false. 2. Initialize two empty hash maps: map_s_t and map_t_s. 3. For each index i from 0 to n-1: a. Get chars s_char = s[i], t_char = t[i]. b. If s_char in map_s_t: if map_s_t[s_char] != t_char, return false. c. Else: if t_char in map_t_s, return false (since t_char already mapped to another s_char). d. Else: set map_s_t[s_char] = t_char and map_t_s[t_char] = s_char. 4. Return true if loop completes. This feeds into the overall problem by providing a complete, efficient solution that handles all edge cases.

examples.trace: Step 1: Lengths both 3, proceed. Initialize map_s_t = {}, map_t_s = {}. Step 2: i=0, s_char='e', t_char='a'. 'e' not in map_s_t, 'a' not in map_t_s. Set map_s_t['e']='a', map_t_s['a']='e'. Step 3: i=1, s_char='g', t_char='d'. 'g' not in map_s_t, 'd' not in map_t_s. Set map_s_t['g']='d', map_t_s['d']='g'. Step 4: i=2, s_char='g', t_char='d'. 'g' in map_s_t, map_s_t['g']='d' matches t_char='d', continue. Step 5: Loop ends, return true.

Step 1: Lengths both 3, proceed. Initialize map_s_t = {}, map_t_s = {}. Step 2: i=0, s_char='f', t_char='b'. 'f' not in map_s_t, 'b' not in map_t_s. Set map_s_t['f']='b', map_t_s['b']='f'. Step 3: i=1, s_char='o', t_char='a'. 'o' not in map_s_t, 'a' not in map_t_s. Set map_s_t['o']='a', map_t_s['a']='o'. Step 4: i=2, s_char='o', t_char='r'. 'o' in map_s_t, map_s_t['o']='a' does not match t_char='r', return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bidirectional Mapping Validation
ladders.desc: Implement a function that checks and updates bidirectional character mappings using two hash maps.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the isomorphic strings problem, focusing on maintaining and validating bidirectional mappings between characters. The intuition is that isomorphism requires each character in one string to map uniquely to a character in the other string, and no two characters can map to the same character. Using two hash maps, map_s_t and map_t_s, we enforce this by checking both directions during iteration. For each pair of characters (s_char, t_char), we first check if s_char is already mapped in map_s_t. If it is, we verify the mapping matches t_char; otherwise, we return false. If s_char is not mapped, we check if t_char is already mapped in map_t_s to prevent multiple s_chars from mapping to the same t_char. If t_char is mapped, return false; otherwise, create new entries in both maps. This ensures one-to-one correspondence. The algorithm runs in O(1) per character check, with overall O(n) time for the full string. Space is O(k) for the maps. This sub-routine feeds into L0 by being the iterative step applied to each character pair. Common mistakes include forgetting to check map_t_s when adding new mappings, leading to incorrect results for cases like 'ab' and 'aa'. Step-by-step: 1. Input: s_char, t_char, map_s_t, map_t_s. 2. If s_char in map_s_t: return (map_s_t[s_char] == t_char). 3. Else: if t_char in map_t_s: return false. 4. Else: set map_s_t[s_char] = t_char and map_t_s[t_char] = s_char; return true. This encapsulates the bidirectional logic needed for the full solution.

examples.trace: Step 1: 'a' not in map_s_t. Step 2: 'b' not in map_t_s. Step 3: Set map_s_t['a']='b', map_t_s['b']='a'. Step 4: Return true.
Step 1: 'a' in map_s_t, map_s_t['a']='c' does not match t_char='b'. Step 2: Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — One-to-One Character Mapping
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the concept of ensuring each character maps uniquely between two sets.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of isomorphic strings is establishing a one-to-one mapping between characters of two strings. This means for every character in string s, there is exactly one corresponding character in string t, and no two characters in s map to the same character in t. The intuition is similar to a bijective function in mathematics: it must be both injective (one-to-one) and surjective (onto). In practice, we enforce this by tracking mappings in both directions. When we encounter a new pair (s_char, t_char), we check if s_char is already mapped; if so, the mapping must match t_char. If s_char is new, we ensure t_char is not already mapped to a different s_char. This bidirectional check prevents collisions and ensures uniqueness. This logic is fundamental because without it, strings like 'ab' and 'aa' would incorrectly be considered isomorphic (with one map, 'a'→'a' and 'b'→'a', but 'a' maps to two different characters). The algorithm involves iterating through strings and applying this check per character. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration, and space is O(k) for storing mappings. This feeds into L1 by providing the conceptual basis for the bidirectional validation sub-routine. Common mistakes include misunderstanding one-to-one as just one-directional or not handling edge cases like empty strings. Step-by-step: 1. For each character pair, maintain forward and backward maps. 2. Check forward map for existing s_char mapping. 3. If new, check backward map for existing t_char mapping. 4. Update both maps if valid. This ensures the mapping is bijective.

examples.trace: Step 1: Pair (a,c): a not mapped, c not mapped, map a→c, c→a. Step 2: Pair (b,d): b not mapped, d not mapped, map b→d, d→b. Step 3: All mappings unique, return true.
Step 1: Pair (a,a): a not mapped, a not mapped, map a→a, a→a. Step 2: Pair (a,b): a is mapped to a, but t_char=b does not match, return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Hash Map Operations for Character Tracking
ladders.desc: Learn to use hash maps to store and retrieve character mappings efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on using hash maps (or dictionaries) to track relationships between characters, which is essential for the isomorphic strings problem. The intuition is that hash maps provide O(1) average-time complexity for insertions, lookups, and updates, making them ideal for maintaining mappings between characters from two strings. We use two hash maps: one to map characters from string s to string t, and another to map from t to s. This allows quick checks to see if a character is already mapped and to what value. For example, map_s_t[s_char] gives the corresponding t_char if it exists. The key operations are: checking if a key exists (e.g., s_char in map_s_t), retrieving a value (map_s_t[s_char]), and inserting a new key-value pair (map_s_t[s_char] = t_char). These operations are fundamental because they enable the bidirectional validation needed for one-to-one mapping. Time complexity for each operation is O(1) on average, leading to efficient overall algorithm. Space complexity is proportional to the number of unique characters. This feeds into L2 by providing the data structure implementation for the core logic. Common mistakes include using lists instead of hash maps (which would be O(n) for lookups) or not initializing maps properly. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty hash maps. 2. For a given s_char, check existence: if s_char in map_s_t. 3. If exists, compare value to t_char. 4. If not exists, check t_char in map_t_s before inserting. 5. Insert using assignment. This block teaches how to leverage hash maps for efficient character correlation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check 'g' in map_s_t? No. Step 2: Check 'd' in map_t_s? No. Step 3: Insert map_s_t['g']='d', map_t_s['d']='g'. New maps: {'e':'a', 'g':'d'} and {'a':'e', 'd':'g'}.
Step 1: Check 'a' in map_s_t? Yes, value is 'b'. Step 2: Compare 'b' to t_char='c', they differ. Step 3: Return false or indicate conflict.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Two Strings Simultaneously
ladders.desc: Practice looping through two strings of equal length and accessing corresponding characters.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating through two strings in parallel, which is a prerequisite for comparing character mappings in the isomorphic strings problem. The intuition is that to check if strings s and t are isomorphic, we need to examine each character pair (s[i], t[i]) for all indices i. This requires a loop that runs from i = 0 to i = n-1, where n is the length of the strings (assuming they are equal; if not, they cannot be isomorphic). During each iteration, we access s[i] and t[i] to get the current characters. This operation is fundamental because it allows us to apply the mapping logic to each pair sequentially. The algorithm is straightforward: use a for-loop with an index variable, and ensure the strings have the same length before iterating. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration, and space is O(1) excluding input storage. This feeds into L3 by providing the iteration framework needed to apply hash map operations across all characters. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, not checking string lengths first, or using different loop types incorrectly. Step-by-step: 1. Check if len(s) == len(t); if not, return false. 2. For i in range(len(s)): a. s_char = s[i], t_char = t[i]. b. Process the pair (e.g., print or compare). This operation teaches how to traverse multiple sequences in sync, a common pattern in string algorithms.

examples.trace: Step 1: Lengths both 3, proceed. Step 2: i=0, s_char='c', t_char='d'. Step 3: i=1, s_char='a', t_char='o'. Step 4: i=2, s_char='t', t_char='g'. Step 5: End loop.
Step 1: len(s)=2, len(t)=5, not equal. Step 2: Return false or indicate mismatch without iteration.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings and Character Access
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of strings, including indexing and character retrieval.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces strings as sequences of characters, which is essential for the isomorphic strings problem. A string is a data structure that holds a sequence of characters, such as letters, digits, or symbols. In programming, strings are typically zero-indexed, meaning the first character is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. To access a character at a specific position, we use indexing: for a string s, s[i] returns the character at index i. For example, if s = "hello", then s[0] is 'h', s[1] is 'e', etc. The length of a string, often obtained with len(s), tells how many characters it contains. This concept is fundamental because in the isomorphic strings problem, we need to compare characters from two strings by their positions, requiring knowledge of how to retrieve them. The intuition is that strings are like arrays of characters, and indexing allows direct access to each element. This feeds into L4 by providing the basic skill needed to iterate through strings. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in some math contexts) or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Step-by-step: 1. Define a string, e.g., s = "abc". 2. Use len(s) to get length 3. 3. Access s[0] for 'a', s[1] for 'b', s[2] for 'c'. 4. Understand that s[3] would cause an error (index out of range). This foundation is crucial for beginners to handle string operations in algorithms.

examples.output: Characters: a, p, p, l, e
examples.trace: Step 1: s[0] = 'a'. Step 2: s[1] = 'p'. Step 3: s[2] = 'p'. Step 4: s[3] = 'l'. Step 5: s[4] = 'e'. Length is 5.
Step 1: t[0] = '1'. Step 2: t[1] = '2'. Step 3: t[2] = '3'. Length is 3.

approaches.name: First Occurrence Index O(n)
approaches.summary: Map each character to the index of its first occurrence in the string. Compare the pattern of first occurrence indices between both strings to determine isomorphism.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is character set size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Isomorphic Strings Using First Occurrence Index
ladders.desc: Complete solution that determines if two strings are isomorphic by comparing patterns of first occurrence indices.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the isomorphic strings problem by transforming each string into a pattern based on where each character first appears. The intuition is that isomorphic strings must have identical character mapping patterns. If we replace each character with the index of its first occurrence in the string, two isomorphic strings will produce identical patterns. For example, in 'egg' and 'add', 'e' and 'a' both appear first at index 0, 'g' and 'd' both appear first at index 1, and the second 'g' and second 'd' both reference index 1. The algorithm works by: 1) Creating a helper function that converts a string to a pattern list where each character is replaced by the index of its first occurrence. 2) Comparing the patterns of both strings for equality. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(k) where k is character set size (at most 256 for ASCII), for storing first occurrence indices. This approach elegantly avoids explicit mapping dictionaries by encoding the mapping in the pattern itself. Common mistakes include: not handling different length strings (should return false), forgetting that mapping must be bijective (1-1), which this approach automatically ensures since patterns compare both directions implicitly when applied to both strings. The connection to L1 is that this level uses the pattern generation function as a key subroutine.

examples.trace: Step 1: Process 'egg'. Initialize empty dictionary for first indices. Character 'e' at index 0: not in dictionary, add {'e': 0}. Pattern list: [0]. Character 'g' at index 1: not in dictionary, add {'g': 1}. Pattern list: [0, 1]. Character 'g' at index 2: already in dictionary with value 1. Pattern list: [0, 1, 1]. Final pattern for 'egg': [0,1,1]. Step 2: Process 'add'. Initialize dictionary. Character 'a' at index 0: not in dictionary, add {'a': 0}. Pattern: [0]. Character 'd' at index 1: not in dictionary, add {'d': 1}. Pattern: [0, 1]. Character 'd' at index 2: already in dictionary with value 1. Pattern: [0, 1, 1]. Final pattern for 'add': [0,1,1]. Step 3: Compare patterns [0,1,1] == [0,1,1] → true.

Step 1: Process 'foo'. Dictionary starts empty. 'f' at index 0: add {'f': 0}, pattern [0]. 'o' at index 1: add {'o': 1}, pattern [0,1]. 'o' at index 2: already mapped to 1, pattern [0,1,1]. Step 2: Process 'bar'. 'b' at index 0: add {'b': 0}, pattern [0]. 'a' at index 1: add {'a': 1}, pattern [0,1]. 'r' at index 2: add {'r': 2}, pattern [0,1,2]. Step 3: Compare [0,1,1] != [0,1,2] → false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate First Occurrence Pattern from String
ladders.desc: Convert a string to a list where each character is replaced by its first occurrence index.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the isomorphic strings solution. The intuition is that we can represent the character mapping pattern of a string by recording where each character first appears. For each character in the string, we check if we've seen it before. If not, we store its current index as its first occurrence. We then append that index to a pattern list. This creates a canonical representation of the string's character relationships. For example, 'aba' becomes [0,1,0] because 'a' first appears at index 0, 'b' at index 1, and the third character 'a' references back to index 0. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty dictionary 'first_index' and empty list 'pattern'. 2) For each index i and character c in string: if c not in first_index, set first_index[c] = i. Append first_index[c] to pattern. 3) Return pattern list. Time complexity is O(n) for scanning the string once. Space complexity is O(k) for the dictionary storing first indices. This subroutine feeds into L0 by being called twice (once per string) and comparing results. Common mistakes: using the current index instead of first occurrence index, not handling empty strings properly (should return empty list). The pattern generation ensures that identical character distributions produce identical patterns, which is why comparing patterns determines isomorphism.

examples.trace: Initialize first_index = {}, pattern = []. Index 0: character 'e' not in first_index, set first_index['e'] = 0, append 0 → pattern [0]. Index 1: character 'g' not in first_index, set first_index['g'] = 1, append 1 → pattern [0,1]. Index 2: character 'g' in first_index with value 1, append 1 → pattern [0,1,1]. Return [0,1,1].

first_index = {}, pattern = []. Index 0: 'a' not in dict, set first_index['a'] = 0, append 0 → [0]. Index 1: 'b' not in dict, set first_index['b'] = 1, append 1 → [0,1]. Index 2: 'a' in dict with value 0, append 0 → [0,1,0]. Return [0,1,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Character Mapping via First Occurrence Indices
ladders.desc: The insight that character isomorphism can be checked by comparing first occurrence patterns.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the conceptual breakthrough: instead of maintaining explicit character-to-character mappings, we can encode the mapping structure by recording where each character first appears in the string. The intuition is that two strings are isomorphic if and only if their character position patterns match. For any character in string s, if it maps to character in string t, then every occurrence of that character in s must correspond to the same character in t. By replacing each character with the index of its first occurrence, we create a pattern that captures this structure. For example, consider 'paper' and 'title'. 'paper' pattern: p(0), a(1), p→0, e(3), r(4) → [0,1,0,3,4]. 'title' pattern: t(0), i(1), t→0, l(3), e(4) → [0,1,0,3,4]. Patterns match, so isomorphic. This works because: 1) If two characters in the same string map to the same index, they must be the same character (ensuring injectivity). 2) Different characters map to different first indices. The algorithm realization is simple: generate pattern lists for both strings and compare equality. Time O(n), space O(k). This logic is implemented in L1 as the pattern generation function. Common mistakes: misunderstanding that this automatically ensures bijection (it does because patterns compare both directions). This approach is elegant because it avoids complex dictionary comparisons.

examples.trace: For 'abc': 'a' first at 0, 'b' at 1, 'c' at 2 → pattern [0,1,2]. For 'def': 'd' first at 0, 'e' at 1, 'f' at 2 → pattern [0,1,2]. Patterns equal, so isomorphic (every character maps uniquely).

For 'aa': 'a' first at 0, second 'a' references 0 → [0,0]. For 'ab': 'a' first at 0, 'b' first at 1 → [0,1]. Patterns differ [0,0] != [0,1], so not isomorphic (in 'aa', same character maps to same, but in 'ab', different characters).

ladders.title: Building Block — Track First Occurrence of Characters in Sequence
ladders.desc: Given a sequence, record the first index where each distinct element appears.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves processing a sequence (like a string) and maintaining a mapping from each distinct element to the first index where it appears. The intuition is that by recording first occurrences, we can later reference them to understand the pattern of repetition. For example, in string 'hello', we note that 'h' appears first at index 0, 'e' at 1, 'l' at 2, and 'o' at 4. When we encounter the second 'l' at index 3, we can refer back to index 2. This building block is essential for L2's pattern generation. The algorithm: initialize empty dictionary. Iterate through sequence with index i. For each element x: if x not in dictionary, set dictionary[x] = i. After processing, the dictionary contains first indices. Time O(n) to traverse sequence, space O(k) for dictionary. This feeds into L2 by being the mechanism to create the pattern list—instead of just storing the dictionary, we use it to output indices as we go. Common mistakes: updating the dictionary for later occurrences (should only set on first), off-by-one errors in indices. This concept is foundational for many algorithms that need to track element positions, like finding duplicates or encoding sequences.

examples.trace: Initialize dict = {}. Index 0: 'b' not in dict, set dict['b'] = 0. Index 1: 'a' not in dict, set dict['a'] = 1. Index 2: 'n' not in dict, set dict['n'] = 2. Index 3: 'a' already in dict (value 1), do nothing. Index 4: 'n' already in dict (value 2), do nothing. Index 5: 'a' already in dict, do nothing. Final dict: {'b':0, 'a':1, 'n':2}.

Dict starts empty. Index 0: 'a' not in dict, set dict['a']=0. Index 1: 'b' not in dict, set dict['b']=1. Index 2: 'r' not in dict, set dict['r']=2. Index 3: 'a' already in dict (0), skip. Index 4: 'c' not in dict, set dict['c']=4. Index 5: 'a' already in dict, skip. Index 6: 'd' not in dict, set dict['d']=6. Index 7: 'a' already, skip. Index 8: 'b' already, skip. Index 9: 'r' already, skip. Index 10: 'a' already, skip. Final dict: {'a':0, 'b':1, 'r':2, 'c':4, 'd':6}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Look Up or Insert in Dictionary with Default
ladders.desc: Check if key exists in dictionary; if not, insert with a value.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves checking for a key in a dictionary and inserting it with a specific value only if it doesn't already exist. The intuition is that dictionaries allow O(1) average-time lookups and insertions, making them ideal for tracking seen elements. In the context of our algorithm, we use this to record the first index of each character: when we encounter a character, we check if it's in the dictionary; if not, we add it with the current index as its value. This ensures each character's first occurrence is stored exactly once. The algorithm pattern: if key not in dict: dict[key] = value. In Python, this can be done with dict.setdefault(key, value) or simply the if-check. Time complexity is O(1) average per operation. Space complexity grows with number of unique keys. This operation feeds into L3 by being the core step in building the first occurrence dictionary. Common mistakes: inserting on every occurrence (which would overwrite), using wrong value (should be current index for first occurrence). This operation is ubiquitous in algorithms for counting, deduplication, and memoization.

examples.trace: Initialize dict = {}. Step 1: character 'a' at index 0. 'a' not in dict → insert dict['a'] = 0. Dict = {'a':0}. Step 2: character 'b' at index 1. 'b' not in dict → insert dict['b'] = 1. Dict = {'a':0, 'b':1}. Step 3: character 'a' at index 2. 'a' is in dict (value 0), so do nothing. Final dict = {'a':0, 'b':1}.

examples.input: Process numbers [5,3,5,7] with indices as values
examples.trace: dict = {}. Index 0: number 5 not in dict → dict[5]=0. Dict {5:0}. Index 1: 3 not in dict → dict[3]=1. Dict {5:0, 3:1}. Index 2: 5 is in dict (value 0), skip. Index 3: 7 not in dict → dict[7]=3. Final dict {5:0, 3:1, 7:3}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Iterate Through String with Index
ladders.desc: Basic loop that goes through each character of a string along with its position.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: traversing a string character by character while keeping track of the current index. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we often need to process each character along with its position (index) in the string. For example, in 'hello', character 'h' is at index 0, 'e' at 1, etc. This foundation is essential for L4 because we need to know the index to store as the first occurrence value. The algorithm: use a for-loop with enumerate() in Python, or a manual index variable. For each step, we have access to both the character and its index. Time complexity is O(n) for visiting n characters. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. This feeds into L4 by providing the index value used in dictionary insertion. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (indices usually start at 0), forgetting to handle empty strings (loop won't execute). This concept is fundamental to almost all string processing algorithms.

examples.trace: Initialize index i = 0. Loop: i=0, character='c'. i=1, character='a'. i=2, character='t'. End after 3 characters.
Step 1: index 0, character 'a'. Step 2: index 1, character 'b'. Done.
